content: Metabolic bariatric surgery encompasses surgical procedures that induce weight loss and improve metabolic comorbidities through restrictive, malabsorptive, and hormonal mechanisms. Major procedures: (1) Roux-en-Y gastric bypass (RYGB) - creates small gastric pouch (~30mL) connected to jejunum, bypassing duodenum; combines restriction and mild malabsorption; most studied with longest track record; (2) Sleeve gastrectomy - removes ~80% of stomach along greater curvature, creating tubular 'sleeve'; primarily restrictive with hormonal effects (reduced ghrelin); now most commonly performed; (3) Biliopancreatic diversion with duodenal switch (BPD/DS) - sleeve gastrectomy plus extensive intestinal bypass; most effective for weight loss but highest nutritional risk; (4) Adjustable gastric banding - largely abandoned due to poor long-term outcomes. Mechanisms beyond restriction/malabsorption: altered gut hormones (increased GLP-1, PYY; decreased ghrelin), bile acid changes, microbiome alterations. Diabetes remission in 60-80% of T2DM patients.
